goal_07.config.title: Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?
goal_07.config.abstract: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet progress remains deeply uneven. While most regions have achieved near-universal coverage, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to lag significantly, particularly its rural communities. Initiatives like Mission 300 aim to extend electricity to 300 million more people in Africa by 2030.
goal_07.config.citation: Fujs, T, Kashiwase, H and Lambrechts, M. 2026. “Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D Mahler, A F Pirlea, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, D Wadhwa and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_07.config.homeFactoidText: people in Sub-Saharan Africa live without electricity today
goal_07.config.homeTagline: Although global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, millions in rural Sub-Saharan Africa have been left behind.
goal_07.content.#1.text: Reliable electricity access is important for improving quality of life. It contributes to increasing comfort, but also supports work productivity, connectivity, education, and the provision of health services. Lack of access to electricity severely limits opportunities to learn, work, and thrive.
goal_07.content.#2.title: People without access to electricity
goal_07.content.#2.label: In 2023, globally
goal_07.content.#3.text: Electricity enables students to study after dark, ensures medical supplies storage and provision, powers irrigation, enables modern industrial production, and creates jobs[reference: job_creation],all essential drivers of economic and social development. Yet, 666 million people still live without electricity, and many more have limited or unreliable supply.
goal_07.content.#4.text: Global gains in access, but uneven progress
goal_07.content.#5.text: Access to electricity has increased almost continuously over the past two decades. But while many regions have achieved near-universal access, large gaps remain.
goal_07.content.#6.title: Electricity access is advancing globally and across all regions
goal_07.content.#6.subtitle: People with access to electricity, by region (% of population)
goal_07.content.#6.y_axis_title: Share of people with access to electricity
goal_07.content.#6.source: World Bank Group. “Access to electricity (% of population.” SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
goal_07.content.#6.visdescription: Line charts describing time series trendlines of electricity access by region and the world.
goal_07.content.#6.keyFactLabel: of the world’s population had access to electricity in 2023.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: Global access to electricity has expanded steadily over the past two decades. By 2023, more than 90 percent of the world’s population had access to electricity—up from 78 percent in 2000.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: Most regions already had very high levels of access in 2000, and had achieved near-universal access to electricity by 2023.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: Two regions, South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa lagged noticeably behind in 2000.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#3.text: But South Asia has since made dramatic progress, from less than 60 percent of the population in 2000 to near universal access by 2021.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#4.text: Sub-Saharan Africa started from an even lower base (around 26 percent in 2000). Although access doubled over the next two decades, only half of the region’s population had electricity by 2023.
goal_07.content.#7.text: While progress has been remarkable, this indicator captures only the most basic level of electricity access. It measures the share of people whose primary source of lighting at home is electricity, whether from the grid, solar home systems, mini-grids, or other stand-alone renewable sources. Having a connection, however, does not guarantee access to reliable, affordable, or sufficient electricity to power appliances and support productive uses.[footnote: To see the different levels of electricity access defined by the Multi-Tier Framework, please see World Bank. 2023. “Access to Universal and Sustainable Electricity: Meeting the Challenge.” In Atlas of Sustainable Development Goals 20203. n] Still, reaching universal access at this basic level is a critical milestone. It is the first step toward ensuring that everyone can benefit from modern energy services.
goal_07.content.#8.text: But has this remarkable progress translated into a decline in the total number of people without electricity access?
goal_07.content.#9.title: There are fewer people without electricity, even as the global population grows
goal_07.content.#9.source: Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP). SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
goal_07.content.#9.note: Regional estimates of population without access to electricity are calculated by World Bank staff.
goal_07.content.#9.visdescription: Chart decomposing global access to electricity by regions, then zooming in on Sub-Saharan Africa, which it decomposes by country.
goal_07.content.#9.keyFactLabel: of the 666 million people without access to electricity live in Sub-Saharan Africa.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#0.text: While the global population has increased by almost 2 billion people since 2000…
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#1.text: … the number of people without electricity halved, falling from 1.3 billion in 2000 to 666 million in 2023.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#2.text: All regions except Sub-Saharan Africa contributed to this rapid decline, particularly South Asia. In 2000, both South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for roughly the same share of people without electricity. But by 2023, South Asia achieved near-universal access, while the number of people without electricity in Sub-Saharan Africa increased. Despite significant progress in access in the region, it was not enough to keep up with population growth.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#3.text: In 2023, Sub-Saharan Africa was home to 581 million people without electricity. This translates to nearly 9 in 10 of the world’s people living without electricity.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#4.text: And just eight countries account for half the world’s  people who still lack access to electricity.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#5.text: Over one-third of the global population without electricity live in the region’s most populous nations: [emphasis: Nigeria], the [emphasis: Democratic Republic of Congo], and [emphasis: Ethiopia] .
goal_07.content.#10.text: Where electricity access still lags: rural Sub-Saharan Africa
goal_07.content.#11.text: Urban areas reached near‑universal electricity access long before rural ones. In 2000, 94 percent of urban residents had electricity, compared with just 66 percent of people in rural areas. By 2023, rural access had climbed to around 83 percent, significantly narrowing the gap.
goal_07.content.#12.text: In most regions, urban and rural access rates have gradually moved closer together. But Sub-Saharan Africa stands out as a persistent outlier. In 2023, only one-in-three rural residents in the region  had access to electricity, compared with more than four-in-five people in urban areas.
goal_07.content.#13.title: Access to electricity is lower in rural areas
goal_07.content.#13.subtitle: People with access to electricity (% of population)
goal_07.content.#13.source: World Bank Group. “Access to electricity (% of population,” “Access to electricity, rural (% of rural population,” “Access to electricity, urban (% of urban population.” SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
goal_07.content.#13.visdescription: Line charts showing the difference in access to electricity between urban and rural areas in different regions and the world.
goal_07.content.#14.text: The persistence of this gap in Sub‑Saharan Africa reflects the higher costs per connection and constraints in affordability in sparsely populated, low‑income rural areas. Homes are widely dispersed, driving up infrastructure costs, while lower electricity use in rural areas limits revenues for providers. Although the underlying economics represents a challenge to service extension into remote rural areas,[reference: wb_electrifying_africa] planning and regulatory shortcomings have also played a role in delaying rural electrification and the wider deployment of off‑grid options.[reference: wb_ssa_access]
goal_07.content.#15.text: Progress in Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Ethiopia and remaining disparities
goal_07.content.#16.text: Of the 30 countries with the largest populations that lack electricity, 24 are in Sub-Saharan Africa. Three —[emphasis: Nigeria], [emphasis: the Democratic Republic of Congo ], and [emphasis: Ethiopia]—account for around one-third of the world’s population without electricity access.
goal_07.content.#17.text: While all three host large populations without electricity access, their national contexts differ considerably. The share of population without[emphasis:  ]access ranges from around 40 percent in Nigeria and 45 percent in Ethiopia to 80 percent in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
goal_07.content.#18.text: Their electrification trajectories also diverge. Between 2015 and 2023, progress in Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo lagged comparable countries, reflecting slow and uneven gains. Ethiopia, on the other hand, made substantial strides, expanding electricity access at nearly twice the pace of its peers (see this explainer for a detailed description of the methodology on progress used in the Atlas).
goal_07.content.#19.title: Ethiopia progress towards universal electrification outpaced Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo
goal_07.content.#19.subtitle: People with access to electricity (% of population)
goal_07.content.#19.source: World Bank staff calculations, based on XXXX 2026 (To be added when working paper available)
goal_07.content.#19.visdescription: Chart showing how the speed of progress in achieving electricity access varies between countries. It focuses on Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Nigeria.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#0.text: In 2015, both Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of Congo lagged Nigeria in electricity access.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#1.text: By2023, the share of Democratic Republic of Congo’s population with access to electricity had increased only slightly, while Ethiopia had almost caught up with Nigeria.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#2.text: Ethiopia is one of the countries that progressed the fastest over the 2015–2023 period. Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo, on the other hand, lagged comparable countries.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#3.text: Now, explore all countries by hovering over the arrow heads or searching for a country in the drop-down menu.
goal_07.content.#20.text: In all three countries, electricity access for rural residents lags urban residents. In the Democratic Republic of Congo, electricity access for rural populations remained extremely low, (around 1 percent) between 2015 and 2023. In Nigeria, the levels were not as low, but little has changed in both rural and urban areas. By contrast, Ethiopia made rapid progress in expanding electricity access in rural areas. From nearly no access, almost half the rural population has  access to electricity today.  In urban areas, access  was already fairly high in 2000, and by 2023, it had reached near universal access. Although gaps remain, the country has  notably reduced the urban-rural access gap.
goal_07.content.#21.title: Rapid rural progress in Ethiopia has reduced the urban–rural gap, while the Democratic Republic of Congo– and Nigeria have made limited progress
goal_07.content.#21.subtitle: People with access to electricity, by place of residence (% of population)
goal_07.content.#21.source: World Bank Group. “Access to electricity, rural (% of rural population,” “Access to electricity, urban (% of urban population.” SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
goal_07.content.#21.visdescription: Three line charts showing electricity access between urban and rural areas in Ethiopia, Nigeria, and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
goal_07.content.#21.keyFactLabel: of rural dwellers in Ethiopia had access to electricity in 2023, up from just 2 percent in 2000.
goal_07.content.#22.text: Electricity access and the infrastructure that supports it vary widely between densely connected urban areas and more isolated rural regions. In cities, people are usually connected to a national electricity grid, while in remote rural areas, many households rely on small solar systems or have no connection. And when households have access to electricity, the quality of supply can vary widely: some have stable power all day, while others experience frequent blackouts, low voltage, or intermittent access .
goal_07.content.#23.text: Let’s examine electricity access at a more granular level, drawing on household surveys and geospatial data to better understand the dynamics of electricity access in Ethiopia and Nigeria, two countries with some of the largest populations without electricity.
goal_07.content.#24.title: Nigeria: uneven and unreliable access
goal_07.content.#24.source: World Bank, Development Data Group . Space2Stats(accessed: February 20, 2026).
goal_07.content.#24.visdescription: Map of nightlights in Nigeria as a proxy for electricity access.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: Electricity access in Nigeria is highly uneven]. Southern zones enjoy relatively high coverage, while the north lags far behind. These disparities reflect the concentration of grid infrastructure and population in the south, along with differences in investment, urbanization, and political stability, as conflict has disrupted large parts of the north in recent years.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: Nighttime lights provide a more granular view of electricity access]. They are a proxy for electricity access and usage. When overlaid on the map, they show where power is being consumed.[footnote: Nighttime lights only capture solar electricity use when backed up by batteries, as the panels do not produce electricity at night. They are therefore a closer proxy of grid connection and use.]
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#2.text: The[emphasis: ]brightest clusters correspond to major cities, such as Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt, where grid access is widespread and power consumption relatively high.Vast rural and peri-urban areas show as weak or dark, signaling either a lack of access or unreliable supply.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#3.text: Let’s focus on Nigeria’s[emphasis:  urban areas]…
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#4.text: … and zoom in onKano. As in most cities, a large majority of households here are connected to the grid. Where bright lights are detected, neighborhoods are likely well served with access to reliable electricity.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#5.text: Filtering out the brightest data points highlights that, even when connected to the grid, [emphasis: large urban and suburban zones may remain underserved, as nighttime lights appear ][emphasis: weak][emphasis: or ][emphasis: dark][emphasis: .]
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#6.text: Although these are densely populated suburban settlements, they still lack highly visible illuminations.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#7.text: For example, this 36 square kilometer cell is estimated to house more than 100,000 people, but remains largely in the dark. This illustrates that, even though most urban areas are connected to the grid, quality of access is not yet universal and [emphasis: grid reliability is far from consistent].
goal_07.content.#25.text: Grid unreliability is a defining feature of electricity access in Nigeria, where  blackouts are frequent and prolonged across much of the country.[reference: nigeria_survey], At national level, households report about seven outages per week, with a typical duration of 12 hours. To cope, many Nigerians alternate between grid power and generators. Petrol and diesel generators are ubiquitous in both urban and rural settings. Although they provide an essential backup, they are financially burdensome and a source of toxic emissions that harm both people and ecosystems.[reference: nigeria_blog]
goal_07.content.#26.text: Like Nigeria, electricity access in Ethiopia is uneven, and urban centers, such as Addis Ababa and the regional capitals have the highest access level through grid connection. In urban centers, 92 percent of the population relied on grid lines as their primary electricity source in 2022.[reference: ethiopia_survey] But even here, access is not universal, as five percent of urban Ethiopians (1.7 million people) do not have access to electricity.
goal_07.content.#27.title: Ethiopia: progress in rural areas
goal_07.content.#27.subtitle: Solar electricity is a key factor of progress in rural areas
goal_07.content.#27.source: World Bank, Development Data Group . Space2Stats(accessed: February 20, 2026).
goal_07.content.#27.visdescription: Map of nighttime lights in Ethiopia as a proxy for electricity access. It focused on access progress in rural areas.
goal_07.content.#27.newly_lit_2023: Dark areas in 2012 that had some night lights in 2023
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#0.text: Let’s focus on Ethiopia’s [emphasis: rural areas only][footnote: Urban areas are left blank to focus on the nighttime lights view in rural areas.][emphasis: ]and take a look at the nighttime lights view of 2012. The areas where at least some light (weak or strong) was detected are shown in light blue, and areas where no nighttime light was detected, in dark blue. While western parts of rural Ethiopia had some nighttime lights coverage in 2012, eastern and southern rural Ethiopia remained largely in the dark.
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#1.text: The power grid lines reached only a few rural areas in 2012. So how did the illuminated rural areas have access to electricity? About 75 percent of the [emphasis: rural population] in Ethiopia with access consumed electricity through[emphasis: solar power.][reference: ethiopia_survey]
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#2.text: Let’s focus on the [emphasis: south-east,] which is more sparsely populated and remained mostly in the dark in 2012[emphasis: .]
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#3.text: By 2023, a lot more nighttime lights could be detected in the southeast. Much of this is due to the [emphasis: rapid uptake of solar electricity][reference: ethiopia_survey], which helped reduce the persistent gap in access to electricity in rural areas.
goal_07.content.#28.text: While access reliability may still be an issue in Ethiopia, the country has made significant improvements , with some regions experiencing a 50 percent decrease in power outages over 2019–22.[reference: ethiopia_survey]
goal_07.content.#30.text: Despite significant global progress in electricity access, major gaps remain. The vast majority of people without electricity are in Sub-Saharan Africa, where 581 million people lacked access in 2023. Even within the region, a small number of countries account for most of this deficit. Spatial inequalities also endure, with rural areas persistently lagging urban centers.
goal_07.content.#31.text: Resources and efforts must therefore be focused on reaching universal access in this region, where the nature of access gaps varies widely, reflecting differences in geography, infrastructure, utility performance, and regulatory environments. Closing the divide will require context-specific approaches that align local conditions with appropriate technical and financing solutions.
goal_07.content.#32.text: The World Bank is working with regional institutions and governments to  help close these gaps. In 2024, the World Bank and African Development Bank launched Mission 300[reference: mission300], a joint initiative to accelerate universal access in Sub-Saharan Africa by providing electricity to 300 million people by 2030, effectively halving the number of people currently without power. Mission 300 will expand grid connections where  this is economically viable, strengthen utilities to improve service quality and financial sustainability, and scale decentralized renewables—such as mini-grids and solar home systems—to reach communities beyond the grid.
goal_07.references.job_creation: Lehr, U. 2023. Occupational Choice and Energy Access – Electricity For More and Better Jobs. World Bank.
goal_07.references.sdg7_1_1: UNSTATS. “SDG Indicator 7.1.1 Metadata (Proportion of population with access to electricity).” SDG Indicators Metadata Repository.
goal_07.references.esmap_data: Original data sources are available in the ESMAP dataset file. For methodological details, see UNSTATS. “SDG Indicator 7.1.1 mMetadata (Proportion of population with access to electricity).” SDG Indicators Metadata Repository.
goal_07.references.nigeria_survey: National Bureau of Statistics. 2024. “Nigeria General Household Survey – Panel (Wave 5) (2023–2024).”
goal_07.references.nigeria_blog: World Bank. 2023. Nigeria to expand access to clean energy for 17.5 million people. World Bank press release, December 15.
goal_07.references.ethiopia_survey: Ethiopian Statistical Service and World Bank. 2023. “Ethiopia Socioeconomic Panel Survey (ESPS) Report - Wave 5, 2021/22.”
goal_07.references.mission300: World Bank. “Mission 300 is Powering Africa.”
goal_07.references.wb_ssa_access: Blimpo, M P and Cosgove-Davies, M. 2019. Electricity Access in Sub-Saharan Africa: Uptake, Reliability, and Complementary Factors for Economic Impact . Africa Development Forum. Washington DC: World Bank Group.
goal_07.references.wb_electrifying_afric: Dabalen, A, Khemani, S, Lang, M and Timilsina, G. 2026. Electrifying Africa's Economic Transformation: What Reforms Should Governments Pursue? World Bank Group.
